ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice noodle roll dish class; no specific belacan stir-fried variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated belacan fried chee cheong fun entry. A0FHR (rice noodle dishes, stir-fried) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Singaporean hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of belacan fried chee cheong fun as served at a Singaporean hawker centre or coffee shop, weighing approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / iDAT database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for belacan fried chee cheong fun as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore food composition database entries for chee cheong fun (plain, stir-fried) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for shrimp paste, bean sprouts, egg, and soy sauce to construct a weighted composite per 100 g. The input record values (energy 177 kcal, protein 5.1 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 16.6 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are not available in the HPB composite for this dish and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles / rice vermicelli analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for belacan fried chee cheong fun. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles / fresh rice rolls (GI ~61–72, mean ~68, glucose reference). The stir-frying process with oil and protein (egg, shrimp paste) modestly lowers the effective GI compared to plain boiled rice noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 58.1) / 100 ≈ 39.5, rounded to 39.7 using 16.6 g/100 g × 350 g serving = 58.1 g carbohydrate per plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 514 mg per 100 g — driven by belacan (shrimp paste), soy sauce, and optional lap cheong (Chinese sausage)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, request reduced belacan and soy sauce, omit lap cheong, and restrict portion to no more than 150 g to keep sodium intake below 770 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined rice noodle base with medium-high GI (~68) and high glycemic load (~39.7 per standard 350 g plate); low dietary fibre at 1.1 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 150–200 g, increase the proportion of bean sprouts to add fibre and slow glucose absorption, and omit added sugar from the stir-fry preparation to reduce postprandial blood glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (514 mg/100 g), moderate saturated fat (2.9 g/100 g), cholesterol (51.4 mg/100 g), and optional processed meat (lap cheong) contributing additional saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high sodium content and the presence of processed meat ingredients; if consumed, omit lap cheong, reduce soy sauce and belacan, and keep the portion to 150 g or less.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (514 mg/100 g) worsening tissue oedema; optional lap cheong is a pro-inflammatory processed pork product rich in arachidonic acid and sodium nitrates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should omit lap cheong entirely, reduce belacan and soy sauce to lower sodium, and monitor tolerance to chilli (nightshade) as it may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Belacan Fried Chee Cheong Fun (马来盏炒猪肠粉) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of smooth, silky rice noodle rolls (chee cheong fun) stir-fried in a wok over high heat with a pungent, umami-rich belacan (fermented shrimp paste) sauce. The dish typically includes crunchy bean sprouts, scrambled or folded egg, dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chilli, and may optionally contain lap cheong (Chinese sausage) or sliced fish cake. The result is a savoury, slightly smoky, and intensely flavoured noodle dish with contrasting textures — soft noodle rolls against crisp bean sprouts. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 177 kcal, 5.1 g protein, 10.0 g fat, 16.6 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 514 mg sodium. The high sodium content from belacan and soy sauce makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The refined rice noodle base carries a medium-to-high glycemic index (~68) and a high glycemic load per standard serving (~39.7), making portion control essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not halal-certified in its traditional form due to the optional inclusion of pork-derived lap cheong. It is a staple of Singaporean Chinese hawker culture, commonly eaten as a breakfast or lunch item.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for belacan fried chee cheong fun as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for chee cheong fun (stir-fried) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for key condiment ingredients. The input record values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles/fresh rice rolls. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite dish with no exact approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.1 g/100 g based on the input record value of 0.06 g/100 g (rounded to 0.1); this is a trace level consistent with cooking oil used in stir-frying and is not naturally zero — it reflects minor trans-fat content from the cooking process. 3. The dish is not halal-certified in its traditional form due to optional lap cheong (pork sausage); halal variants exist at certified hawker stalls where lap cheong is omitted. 4. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fresh rice noodles/rice rolls; no direct GI measurement for belacan fried chee cheong fun was found in the approved GI literature. 5. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific dish.
